DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-12-2024 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. CONOR WALSH, Esq. of Countrywide Trial Lawyer, APLC (tel: 213-493-6003) for Plaintiffs.
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CARINA TAM's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

Matter not argued/contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Carina Tam's demurrer to plaintiffs Deynis Aguilar and Yesi Aguilar's complaint is sustained with leave to amend.

Tam allegedly crashed into plaintiffs' vehicle while driving and fled the scene. Plaintiffs sued for (1) general negligence, (2) motor vehicle, (3) negligence per se, and (4) intentional tort (presumably an intentional infliction of emotional distress ("IIED") claim). Tam demurs to the third and fourth causes of action.

First, Tam argues plaintiffs fail to state a cause of action for negligence per se based on Tam's alleged violation of Vehicle Code 2001(a). (CCP 430.10(e).) Indeed, negligence per se is an evidentiary doctrine, not a separate cause of action. (See Sierra-Bay Fed. Land Bank Assn. v. Superior Court (1991) 227 Cal.App.3d 318, 333 ["[I]t is the tort of negligence, and not the violation of the statute itself, which entitles a plaintiff to recover civil damages"].) Plaintiffs concede this point by failing to address it in their opposition.
Second, Tam argues plaintiffs fail to allege facts sufficient to support their IIED claim. (CCP 430.10(e).) An IIED claim requires "(1) extreme and outrageous conduct by the defendant with the intention of causing, or reckless disregard of the probability of causing, emotional distress; (2) the plaintiff's suffering severe or extreme emotional distress; and (3) actual and proximate causation of the emotional distress by the defendant's outrageous conduct. [Citations.]" (Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1050.) The outrageous conduct must be so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized society. (Id.) Merely fleeing the scene of a traffic accident, without more, falls short of extreme and outrageous conduct. Further, "[s]evere emotional distress [is] emotional distress of such substantial quantity or enduring quality that no reasonable man in a civilized society should be expected to endure it." (Fletcher v. Western Life Insurance Co. (1970) 10 Cal.App.3d 376, 397.) Plaintiffs' "disturbing uncertainty as to how their expenses would be paid without Defendants' insurance or contact information" does not meet this high bar.

In sum, the demurrer to the third cause of action for negligence per se and fourth cause of action for IIED is sustained with leave to amend.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RCE)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-12-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. CONOR WALSH, Esq. of Countrywide Trial Lawyer, APLC (tel: 213-493-6003) for Plaintiffs.
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CARINA TAM's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter not argued/contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Carina Tam's motion to strike punitive damages references in plaintiffs Deynis Aguilar and Yesi Aguilar's complaint is granted with leave to amend.

Punitive damages require clear and convincing evidence the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) The elements of fraud are (1) a misrepresentation (false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure), (2) knowledge of its falsity, (3) intent to induce reliance, (4) actual and justifiable reliance, and (5) resulting damage. (Berry v. Frazier (2023) 90 Cal.App.5th 1258, 1268.) Here, plaintiffs contend Tam's fraudulent conduct is sufficient to support punitive damages. Specifically, plaintiffs argue that Tam fraudulently concealed her involvement in the accident by fleeing the scene and misrepresenting her involvement to police officers days after the accident. (Opp. 7:1-6.) Plaintiffs fail to plead essential elements of fraud, such as how Tam induced their actual and justifiable reliance. Further, none of plaintiffs' allegations rise to the level of malice or oppression.

In sum, the request to strike punitive damages is granted.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RCE)
